hook: You already know that different estimators can have different variances — but how do you know when you've found the *best possible* one? There's a fundamental limit to how precisely any unbiased estimator can work, and most statistics courses never reveal where that wall comes from. In this unit, you'll discover the deep mathematical machinery that tells you exactly how good an estimator can be, and how to actually build one that achieves it.
concept: Every topic here circles the same question: how do we extract the maximum amount of information from data to estimate parameters as efficiently as possible? The unifying idea is *statistical efficiency* — formally captured through the interplay of Fisher information, sufficiency, and optimal unbiased estimation.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Fisher Information and the Score Function
topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to quantify how much information a sample carries about an unknown parameter by computing the score function and Fisher information $I(\theta)$.
Master the fundamental inequality $\mathrm{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq 1/I(\theta)$ that establishes an absolute floor on the variance of any unbiased estimator.
Apply the concepts of sufficiency and completeness to identify statistics that capture all parameter-relevant information in the data without redundancy.
Use the Rao-Blackwell theorem to systematically improve estimators and construct the Uniformly Minimum Variance Unbiased Estimator (UMVUE) — the best unbiased estimator that can exist.
Discover how the exponential family provides a unified framework in which sufficient statistics, Fisher information, and UMVUEs arise naturally and can be computed with elegant efficiency.
